IRN: Iranians eat more ice cream per person than almost any country on Earth. In the DESERT. They've been making ice cream since 400 BC.
NGA: Nollywood (Nigeria's movie industry) makes more movies per year than Hollywood. More. Than. Hollywood.
GBR: There's a law in Scotland that says if someone knocks on your door and needs to use your bathroom, you HAVE to let them in.
IRL: There are no snakes in Ireland. Zero. Legend says St. Patrick chased them all into the sea, but the truth is there never were any since the Ice Age.
USA: There's a town in Alaska where the mayor was a cat. His name was Stubbs and he was mayor for 20 years.
JPN: There's an island in Japan called Okunoshima that's completely taken over by hundreds of wild rabbits. You can just walk around covered in bunnies.
EGY: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when their pet cat died. That's how much they loved cats.
BRA: There's a prison in Brazil where inmates can reduce their sentence by pedaling stationary bikes that power streetlights in the city.
MEX: Mexico City is sinking. Like, actually sinking into the ground. About 20 inches per year. It was built on top of a lake.
AUS: There was a war in Australia. Against emus. The birds. THE EMUS WON.
ITA: There's a town in Italy with a free wine fountain. It runs 24/7 and anyone can drink from it.
KEN: Kenyan runners are so fast that a Kenyan has won the Boston Marathon more times than any other country on Earth. The altitude they train at gives them superhuman lungs.
FRA: It's technically illegal in France to name your pig Napoleon. The law has been on the books for over 200 years.
IND: There's a temple in India where rats are considered sacred. Thousands of them. People drink milk alongside the rats. On purpose.
ISL: Icelanders believe in elves. Seriously. Over half the population won't deny that elves might exist, and roads have been rerouted to avoid disturbing elf homes.
CHN: There are eggs in China that are boiled in the urine of young boys. It's called Virgin Boy Eggs and it's considered a local delicacy in one city.
RUS: There are more TIME ZONES in Russia than in any other country. Eleven of them. When it's bedtime in one part, people in another part are just waking up.
CAN: Canada takes maple syrup SO seriously that there is a STRATEGIC MAPLE SYRUP RESERVE. Like an oil reserve. But for syrup.
DEU: It's illegal to run out of gas on the Autobahn in Germany. If your car stops because you didn't fill up, YOU get in trouble, not the car.
ESP: There's a festival in Spain called La Tomatina where an entire town throws 150,000 tomatoes at each other for one hour. On purpose.
KOR: In South Korea, your age used to change on January 1st, not on your birthday. Everyone in the country got one year older on the same day.
THA: It's illegal to step on money in Thailand because the King's face is on it, and stepping on the King's face is a serious offense.
TUR: Istanbul is the only city in the world that sits on two continents. Half is in Europe. Half is in Asia. You can eat breakfast in Europe and lunch in Asia.
ZAF: South Africa has a penguin colony. In AFRICA. African penguins live on the beaches near Cape Town and waddle around like they own the place.
ARG: There's a glacier in Argentina called Perito Moreno that makes a sound like thunder when chunks of ice break off. You can hear it from miles away.
PER: The Incas invented freeze-dried food. They would leave potatoes out in the cold mountain air to freeze, then stomp the water out with their feet. Astronauts use the same basic idea today.
NOR: There's a town in Norway called Longyearbyen where it's actually ILLEGAL TO DIE. If you're about to die, they fly you to the mainland.
GRC: In Greece, nodding your head means no and shaking your head means yes. The opposite of everywhere else.
NZL: New Zealand has more sheep than people. About 5 sheep for every 1 person. The sheep outnumber humans by millions.
IDN: There's a volcano in Indonesia that shoots BLUE FIRE. Electric blue flames pour out of it at night. It looks like something from another planet.
COL: There's a river in Colombia called Cano Cristales that turns bright red, yellow, green, blue, and black depending on the season. They call it the River of Five Colors.
ARM: Armenia was the first country in the world to adopt Christianity as its official religion. In the year 301 AD. Before Rome. Before everyone.
POL: There's a forest in Poland where every tree is bent at a 90-degree angle near the base. Nobody knows why. Scientists still argue about it.
UKR: There's an abandoned city in Ukraine called Pripyat near Chernobyl where a Ferris wheel has been standing still for 40 years, slowly being taken over by trees.
CUB: Until 2011, you needed government permission to buy a car in Cuba. Most cars on the road are from the 1950s and people keep them running with homemade parts.
CHL: Chile's Atacama Desert is so dry that NASA tests Mars rovers there because it's the closest thing to Mars on Earth.
PAK: Pakistan has a place where three of the world's mightiest mountain ranges meet: the Himalayas, Karakoram, and Hindu Kush. The only place on Earth where this happens.
DNK: LEGO was invented here and the name comes from Danish words meaning play well. The LEGO factory produces 36 billion bricks per year.
FIN: Finns have a word called kalsarikannit that means sitting at home in your underwear with no intention of going out. They made a word for it.
SWE: Sweden has a hotel made entirely of ice. Every year it melts, and every year they rebuild it from scratch.
NLD: The Dutch are the tallest people on Earth. The average man is over 6 feet tall. Scientists still argue about why.
CHE: Switzerland has enough nuclear bunkers to shelter its ENTIRE population. Every person has a spot. Just in case.
TWN: Here's the wildest thing in world politics: Taiwan has its own president, its own military, its own passport, its own money, and its own laws. It acts like a country in EVERY way. But most of the world doesn't officially call it a country because China says 'Taiwan is part of China' and China is so powerful that most countries go along with it to avoid a fight. Taiwan just keeps doing its own thing anyway.
XKX: Kosovo declared independence from Serbia in 2008 and over 100 countries said 'Welcome to the club!' But Serbia said 'Absolutely not, you're still ours.' Russia and China back Serbia. The US and most of Europe back Kosovo. So Kosovo has its own government and its own flag but can't join the United Nations because the big powers can't agree on whether it's real.
PSE: Palestine is one of the most complicated situations on the planet. Palestinians have lived on this land for generations. Israel was created in 1948 and both peoples claim the same land. Palestine has observer status at the United Nations (like being allowed to watch but not vote). More than 140 countries recognize Palestine as a state, but some of the most powerful countries don't. People on both sides want peace, but agreeing on how to share the land has been the hardest problem in modern diplomacy.
HKG: Hong Kong was British for 156 years. In 1997, Britain handed it back to China, but with a deal: China promised Hong Kong could keep its own freedoms for 50 years (until 2047). Hong Kong had free speech, its own courts, and open internet, things mainland China doesn't have. In 2019-2020, huge protests erupted because people felt China was breaking the deal early. China passed a security law and many of those freedoms have been restricted. The clock is ticking toward 2047.
CYN: Northern Cyprus is the world's loneliest country. After a war in 1974, the island of Cyprus was split in two. The north declared itself a country but ONLY Turkey recognizes it. Not a single other country on Earth. The capital Nicosia has a wall running through its center, making it the last divided capital in the world. People cross between the two sides now, but the division remains.
ESH: Western Sahara is Africa's last colony, depending on who you ask. Morocco controls most of it and says it's part of Morocco. The Sahrawi people want their own country. The United Nations has been trying to organize a vote on independence since 1991. It still hasn't happened. Many Sahrawis live in refugee camps in Algeria, waiting for a home country that may never come.
GRL: Greenland belongs to Denmark but desperately wants more independence. It already runs most of its own affairs. In 2019, the US President tried to BUY Greenland from Denmark and the Danish prime minister called the idea 'absurd.' Greenlanders were not amused. But Greenland does have something everyone wants: rare minerals, oil, and a LOT of melting ice that's opening up new shipping routes as the climate changes.
PRI: Puerto Ricans are US citizens. They serve in the US military. They pay some federal taxes. But they CANNOT vote for president and they have no voting members in Congress. Puerto Rico has voted on whether to become a state several times and the answer keeps being 'yes please,' but Congress hasn't acted on it. It's one of the biggest representation gaps in any democracy.
GUM: Guam has been a US territory since 1898 and Chamorros have been US citizens since 1950, but like Puerto Rico, they can't vote for president. The island is basically a giant military base: about a quarter of the island is US military property. Guam is closer to Tokyo than to Honolulu, which makes it one of the most strategically important dots on the US map.
ASM: American Samoa is the only US territory where people born there are NOT automatically US citizens. They're 'US nationals,' which means they can live and work in the US but can't vote. People have been fighting in court to change this. Meanwhile, American Samoa produces more NFL players per capita than any other place on Earth.
SJM: Svalbard has one of the weirdest legal situations on Earth. It belongs to Norway, but thanks to a 1920 treaty, citizens of ANY country that signed the treaty can live and work there. No visa needed. So there's a Thai restaurant, a Russian mining town, and scientists from all over, all living together in the Arctic with polar bears.
COK: The Cook Islands are one of the most confusing political entities on Earth. They govern themselves completely, issue their own passports, and sign their own treaties. But they're in 'free association' with New Zealand, and Cook Islanders are New Zealand citizens. Most countries don't recognize the Cook Islands as a fully independent country, but they act like one.
SXM: Sint Maarten and Saint Martin sit on the same tiny island with NO fence or wall between them. You can walk from the Dutch side to the French side without showing any ID. One side uses euros, the other uses guilders. One side drives on the right, the other... also drives on the right. It's the smallest island in the world shared by two countries.
NCL: New Caledonia held THREE independence referendums (2018, 2020, 2021) on whether to leave France. The first two said stay, the third was boycotted by independence supporters who said COVID made it unfair. So New Caledonia is still French, but nobody is satisfied. The indigenous Kanak people have been fighting for independence for decades.
